word_list.word: the grass is (always|) greener on the other side (of the fence|)
word_list.pronunciation: ðə ɡɹˈæs ɪz ˈɔːlweɪz ɡɹˈiːnɚɹ ɑːnðɪ ˈʌðɚ sˈaɪd ʌvðə fˈɛns

senses.definition: used to say that people often think that other situations or circumstances are better than their own, even if they are not

examples.x: She thinks life will be easier in another city, but the grass is always greener on the other side.
He's been unhappy at work, thinking the grass is always greener on the other side of the fence.
The grass is always greener on the other side, but it's important to appreciate what you have.
You may think another relationship would be better, but remember, the grass is always greener on the other side.
Sometimes, people believe the grass is greener on the other side, but they don't realize that every job has its challenges.

word_list.word: the golden age was never the present age
word_list.pronunciation: ðə ɡˈoʊldən ˈeɪdʒ wʌz nˈɛvɚ ðə pɹˈɛzənt ˈeɪdʒ

senses.definition: used to imply that people tend to idealize the past as a time of greater prosperity, happiness, and virtue, and overlook the flaws and challenges of that time

examples.x: My parents tell me how toys and games were simpler and more enjoyable in their childhood - the golden age was never the present age, and they don't see the same appeal in modern technology.

word_list.word: a prophet is not without honor save in his own country
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ pɹˈɑːfɪt ɪz nˌɑːt wɪðˌaʊt ˈɑːnɚ sˈeɪv ɪn hɪz ˈoʊn kˈʌntɹi

senses.definition: used to suggest that a person's gifts and talents may be overlooked or undervalued by those who are familiar with them

word_list.word: better a small fish than an empty dish
word_list.pronunciation: bˈɛɾɚɹ ɐ smˈɔːl fˈɪʃ ðˌænən ˈɛmpti dˈɪʃ

senses.definition: used to imply that it is better to have something rather than nothing, and that one should appreciate and make the most of what they have

word_list.word: half a loaf is better than (no bread|none)
word_list.pronunciation: hˈæf ɐ lˈoʊf ɪz bˈɛɾɚ ðɐn nˈoʊ bɹˈɛd nˈʌn

senses.definition: used to imply that having something, even if it is not as much as one would like or need, is better than having nothing at all

examples.x: I can't afford the latest smartphone, but I found a used one that's still in good condition - half a loaf is better than no bread, so I bought it.

word_list.word: a good dog deserves a good bone
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ ɡˈʊd dˈɑːɡ dɪzˈɜːvz ɐ ɡˈʊd bˈoʊn

senses.definition: used to emphasize that someone who has done a good job or performed well deserves to be rewarded or recognized appropriately

examples.x: After working overtime and completing the project ahead of schedule, the team was given a bonus because a good dog deserves a good bone.

word_list.word: cow knows not what her tail is worth (till|until) she has lost it
word_list.pronunciation: kˈaʊ nˈoʊz nˌɑːt wˌʌt hɜː tˈeɪl ɪz wˈɜːθ tˈɪl ʌntˈɪl ʃiː hɐz lˈɔst ɪt

senses.definition: used to imply that people often do not recognize the true worth of something or someone until it is lost or no longer available

word_list.word: a blind man would be glad to see (it|)
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ blˈaɪnd mˈæn wʊd biː ɡlˈæd tə sˈiː ɪt

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of appreciating what one has, as something that seems insignificant to one person may be highly valued by someone who lacks it

examples.x: When we find ourselves complaining about our circumstances, we should remember that a blind man would be glad to see.

word_list.word: health is not valued (till|until) sickness comes
word_list.pronunciation: hˈɛlθ ɪz nˌɑːt vˈæljuːd tˈɪl ʌntˈɪl sˈɪknəs kˈʌmz

senses.definition: used to suggest that people often fail to appreciate the value of good health until they become sick, emphasizing the importance of valuing and prioritizing one's health

examples.x: I didn't appreciate the little things my body could do, like running and jumping, until I was injured and couldn't do them anymore - health is not valued till sickness comes, and I miss my old abilities.

word_list.word: a forced kindness deserves no thanks
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ fˈoːɹst kˈaɪndnəs dɪzˈɜːvz nˈoʊ θˈæŋks

senses.definition: used to imply that insincere or forced acts of kindness, done either against one's will or for self-serving purposes, are not deserving of appreciation or gratitude

examples.x: The landlord finally made some repairs in the apartment, but it was only after several complaints and threats of legal action, and a forced kindness deserves no thanks.

word_list.word: blessings brighten as they take their flight
word_list.pronunciation: blˈɛsɪŋz bɹˈaɪʔn̩ æz ðeɪ tˈeɪk ðɛɹ flˈaɪt

senses.definition: used to suggest that people often appreciate the value of something only after it is gone or no longer available

examples.x: It's easy to take our health for granted, but blessings brighten as they take their flight, and we don't realize how much we had until it's gone.

word_list.word: Christmas comes but once a year
word_list.pronunciation: kɹˈɪsməs kˈʌmz bˌʌt wˈʌns ɐ jˈɪɹ

senses.definition: used to imply that some things are rare or happen only on special occasions, and so one should appreciate and make the most of these special moments while they last

examples.x: I know it's expensive, but going on a vacation with my family is something that we can only do once in a while, like Christmas comes but once a year, so we should make the most of it.

word_list.word: distance lends enchantment to the view
word_list.pronunciation: dˈɪstəns lˈɛndz ɛntʃˈæntmənt tə ðə vjˈuː

senses.definition: used to imply that distance can make things appear more attractive or desirable, emphasizing the idea that one's perception of something can be influenced by one's separation from it

examples.x: I miss my college days and the carefree life I had, but distance lends enchantment to the view, and I forget about the stress and struggles that came with it.

word_list.word: eaten bread is (soon|) forgotten
word_list.pronunciation: ˈiːʔn̩ bɹˈɛd ɪz sˈuːn fɚɡˈɑːʔn̩

senses.definition: used to imply that people tend to forget the efforts or sacrifices that were made to provide for them once they have obtained or achieved something

examples.x: I helped my coworker with a project, but now that they've gotten all the credit, eaten bread is forgotten, and they don't acknowledge my contribution.

word_list.word: if there were no clouds, we should not enjoy the sun
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf ðɛɹwˌɜː nˈoʊ klˈaʊdz wiː ʃˌʊd nˌɑːt ɛndʒˈɔɪ ðə sˈʌn

senses.definition: used to suggest that experiencing challenges or difficulties can enhance one's appreciation of positive experiences

word_list.word: misfortunes tell us what fortune is
word_list.pronunciation: mɪsfˈɔːɹtʃənz tˈɛl ˌʌs wˌʌt fˈɔːɹtʃən ɪz

senses.definition: used to imply that experiencing setbacks or difficulties can help one appreciate the good things in life and recognize the true value of fortune

examples.x: Being betrayed by a friend made me appreciate the loyalty and trustworthiness of my other friends - misfortunes tell us what fortune is, and I won't overlook the importance of loyalty in my relationships.

word_list.word: think not on what you lack as much as on what you have
word_list.pronunciation: θˈɪŋk nˌɑːt ˌɑːn wˌʌt juː lˈæk æz mˈʌtʃ æz ˌɑːn wˌʌt juː hˈæv

senses.definition: used to suggest that one should focus on being grateful for what they have rather than dwelling on what they lack

word_list.word: you never miss the water till the well runs dry
word_list.pronunciation: juː nˈɛvɚ mˈɪs ðə wˈɔːɾɚ tˈɪl ðə wˈɛl ɹˈʌnz dɹˈaɪ

senses.definition: used for imply that people often fail to appreciate the value of something or someone until they are no longer available or accessible

word_list.word: youth is wasted on the young
word_list.pronunciation: jˈuːθ ɪz wˈeɪstᵻd ɑːnðə jˈʌŋ

senses.definition: used to imply that young people often lack the perspective and experience to fully appreciate their youth, and that they recognize its value only when it has passed

examples.x: Jack's father told him that youth is wasted on the young when he saw him wasting his time on video games instead of exploring the world and trying new things.

word_list.word: go abroad and you will hear news of home
word_list.pronunciation: ɡˌoʊ ɐbɹˈɔːd ænd juː wɪl hˈɪɹ nˈuːz ʌv hˈoʊm

senses.definition: used to imply that traveling to new places can give a person a new perspective on their own home and can help them appreciate it more

examples.x: I never understood the meaning behind the saying 'go abroad and you will hear news of home', until I traveled to Europe and realized how much I missed the simple things about my hometown.
